title: American Children's Screen Time: Diminished Returns of Household Income in Black Families.
abstract: While increased household income is associated with overall decreased screen time for children, less is known about the effect of racial variation on this association. According to Minorities' Diminished Returns (MDRs) theory, family income and other economic resources show weaker association with children's developmental, behavioral, and health outcomes for racialized groups such as black families, due to the effect of racism and social stratification. In this study, we investigated the association, by race, between family income and children's screen time, as a proxy of screen time. This longitudinal study followed 15,022 American children aged 9-11 over a 1-year period. The data came from the baseline of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study. The independent variable was family income, and it was categorized as a three-level nominal variable. The dependent variable, screen time, was a continuous variable. Ethnicity, gender, parental education, and marital status were the covariates. The results showed that family income was inversely associated with children's screen time. However, there was a weaker inverse association seen in black families when compared with white families. This was documented by a significant statistical interaction between race and family income on children's screen time. Diminished association between family income and children's screen time for black families, compared with white families, is similar to MDRs and reflects a health risk to high-income black children. In a society where race and skin color determine opportunities and treatment by society, children from middle class black families remain at risk across multiple domains. We should not assume that income similarly promotes the health of all racial and ethnic groups. Addressing health and behavioral inequalities requires interventions that go beyond equalizing socioeconomic resources for black families. Marginalization, racism, and poverty interfere with the normal family income-related development of American children.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study baseline data, N=15,022 American children aged 9-11 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as parent-reported average weekday hours of recreational screen use (continuous variable). Described as proxy for screen time behavior.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects regression models with interaction terms (Model 1: family income + race; Model 2: added interaction of household income × race). Regression coefficients, SE, 95% CI, and p-values reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (b) with standard error, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values reported. Example: Income 50-100K: b=-0.60, p<0.001; Income >100K: b=-1.20, p<0.001; Race (Black): b=1.32, p<0.001; Interaction Income (>100K) × Race (Black): b=0.74, p=0.003

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional secondary analysis using ABCD baseline data with mixed-effects regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Information journal (MDPI), original empirical research article

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis (not longitudinal/prospective). Although screen time is the dependent variable and predictors are primarily demographic (family income, race, sociodemographic covariates), FT-EC7 explicitly requires LONGITUDINAL/PROSPECTIVE design. E14 applies instead: sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM in cross-sectional context = MET inclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses baseline data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, which followed 15,022 American children aged 9-11 years.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures children's screen time (continuous variable, reported in hours on typical weekdays by parents). Screen time is a specific smartphone/media use variable per FT-IC2 definition.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports mixed-effects regression models (Model 1 and Model 2) with screen time as outcome and family income + race as predictors. Provides regression coefficients (b), standard errors, 95% CIs, and p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: Model 1 for income ≥50K-<100K: b = -0.60, p < 0.001; for income >100K: b = -1.20, p < 0.001. Model 2 interaction terms also reported with coefficients and p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis: longitudinal observational study examining associations between family income (by race) and children's screen time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Information 2020, 11, 538, not a preprint or duplicate.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the dependent variable (outcome). Predictors are demographic/contextual only (family income, race, sex, parental education, marital status). However, the analyses include both cross-sectional and longitudinal components, and interactions between race and income. Per FT-EC7 rule: it applies ONLY when phone/SM is DV in EVERY analysis AND predictors are ONLY demographic AND ALL analyses are LONGITUDINAL. Since cross-sectional analyses are included (Model 1/2 appear to be cross-sectional at baseline), FT-EC7 does NOT apply per exception rule 'cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as DV'.
